items.title: Is China’s economy worse than it lets on? | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<html><head></head><body>Beijing is again targeting high growth for 2025, despite ongoing economic problems including low consumer demand and stagnating prices. Logan Wright with research firm Rhodium Group breaks down where China’s numbers stray from reality and what’s to expect for one of the world’s largest economies in 2025.

items.title: Foreign policy under Trump: A new era of land-grabs? | To the point
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<html><head></head><body>Donald Trump is headed back to the White House, and already it appears it won’t be business-as-usual for US foreign policy.

What will “America First” mean for the world? Some hope he’ll succeed where his predecessor failed to find lasting peace in Ukraine and the Middle East.

But Trump's latest comments about annexing Greenland and Canada have cast doubt on the United States’ reliability as an ally.

What’s behind Trump’s apparent expansionist ambitions? 

Our guests:

Thorsten Benner, Co-founder and director of the Global Public Policy Institute in Berlin.

Moritz Gathmann, Foreign correspondent for the German weekly magazine, Stern.

Melinda Crane, American journalist 


00:00 Opening

01:50 As Europeans we have to get our priorities straight

02:30 Trump has a short focus on topics

03:40 Trump behaves like a businessman making maximum demands

04:05 Trump is a dealmaker and a dealbreaker

04:40 Film

06:20 Trump is all about bluster

06:40 He makes outrageous claims

07:30 Trump operates on the basis of instincts

07:55 It´s all about power

08:54 There are strategic interests in the Arctic

10:15 People in Greenland want the best deal for themselves

10:45 Will Trump behave like a bully?

11:50 Trump admires Putin

12:05 He also admires Xi Jinping

12:50 Undermining the international rule of law

13:45 The U.S. won’t save us anymore

15:30 Until a few months ago the Europeans thought Trump was an accident

15:55 Trump’s second term will take us into new waters

16:50 Film

18:40 Europeans will spend more for defense

20:00 It can be a push for the Europeans

21:09 What will the strategy on Ukraine be?

23:30 Europeans have to invest in their own security

24:10 Will Trump undermine international rules?


For more news go to: http://www.dw.com/en/

items.title: Can Europe survive or thrive in the new world order? | Business Beyond
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<html><head></head><body>Europe's economic future is at stake. It has fallen far behind the US on multiple metrics and is not productive or innovative enough to close the gap. Now the return of Donald Trump to the White House has raised more existential questions. Can the EU do what is needed to secure a prosperous, democratic future for its citizens? In this episode of Business Beyond, we put the key questions to Eurogroup President Paschal Donohoe, former Italian prime minister Enrico Letta and Martin Wolf, the Financial Times chief economic commentator for almost 30 years.

For more from the experts we spoke to:
Mr Paschal Donohoe, Eurogroup President and Minister for Public Expenditure and Reform of Ireland - https://www.consilium.europa.eu/en/eurogroup/president/biography/

Enrico Letta, Former Prime Minister of Italy and current Dean of IE University’s School of Politics, Economics and Global Affairs in Madrid, Spain - https://www.ie.edu/school-politics-economics-global-affairs/news/enrico-letta-former-italian-prime-minister-appointed-dean-ie-school-politics-economics-global-affairs/

Martin Wolf, Chief Economics Commentator at the Financial Times, former World Bank economist - https://www.ft.com/martin-wolf

And for more Business Beyond episodes: https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLT6yxVwBEbi0AKJXT8tseRoYHctKpD1Fj

Chapters:
00:00 Introduction 
01:41 Trump's America: Europe's Dilemma 
04:34 The Single Market and Fragmentation 
08:33 Innovation and Investment 
13:34 Taking Action: The Letta and Draghi reports 
16:19 Interview with Paschal Donohoe 
26:07 Conclusion
28:48 Ends

#businessbeyond #europe #economy

items.title: Israeli PM Netanyahu refuses to convene security cabinet meeting to approve ceasefire agreement
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<html><head></head><body>Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu's office says his security cabinet will not meet to approve an agreement for a ceasefire in the Gaza Strip and the release of dozens of hostages until Hamas backs down from an attempt to "extort last-minute concessions." Netanyahu's office accuses Hamas of reneging on parts of the agreement. The ceasefire should lead to the release of dozens of hostages held by Hamas, as well as hundreds of Palestinians detained in Israeli jails.

items.title: 'Not in a bad position to rebuild' What does the ceasefire agreement mean for Hamas? | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<html><head></head><body>After months of negotiations, Israel and Hamas have agreed on the terms of a ceasefire that could end the war in Gaza. Even though mediators have confirmed the deal, which is due to take effect on Sunday, Israel's government has yet to ratify it. It says final details are still being worked out. If the deal is implemented, the ceasefire should lead to the release of dozens of hostages held by Hamas, as well as hundreds of Palestinians detained in Israeli jails. That prospect has been welcomed by Palestinians and Israelis. But what does the ceasefire deal mean for the future of Hamas?

#Hamas #Gaza #ceasefire

items.title: What does the Israel-Hamas ceasefire deal look like? | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<html><head></head><body>Qatar's Prime Minister has announced a ceasefire will take effect between Israel and Hamas on January 19. Qatar has been playing a key role as mediator in the ceasefire talks. In the US, both President Joe Biden and President-elect Donald Trump announced a deal has gone through. But Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu says final details still need to be worked out. 
Thousands were celebrating in in the streets in Gaza and Israel. The deal calls for a stop to the hostilities that started with the Hamas-led terror attack on October 7, 2023. Since then, tens of thousands of Palestinians, and hundreds of Israelis have been killed. In the first phase of the ceasefire deal, Hamas will hand over 33 hostages in exchange for hundreds of Palestinian prisoners. 

0:00 Israeli cabinet will vote on Gaza deal on Thursday
0:48 Tania Krämer, DW Jerusalem correspondent
9:25 Laura Blumenfeld, Middle East Analyst, Johns Hopkins University
18:14 Natasha Hall, Senior fellow with the Middle East Program at the Center for Strategic and International Studies, CSIS
25:35 Giorgio Cafieri, Middle East Analyst, CEO and Founder of Gulf State Analytics
31:45 Behnam Ben Taleblu, Senior Fellow, Foundation for Defense of Democracies

items.title: 'Trump doesn't want Ukraine to lose' Ian Bremmer on Donald Trump's geopolitical approach | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<html><head></head><body>Very soon, Donald Trump will be back in the White House as the US President. 
It's a change of power that's expected to have far-reaching consequences for the United States — and also for the rest of the world. Leaders from America's neighbors, here in Europe, and other capitals around the globe are waiting to see what the new US president will do once he returns to the Oval Office.
During Trump's first term in office, he snubbed some of America's traditional European allies, and pursued closer ties with autocrats like the North Korean leader Kim Jong Un. Trump also famously said he wanted a better relationship with Russia. Now, Vladimir Putin's Russia is at war with its democratic neighbor Ukraine, and both sides are pinning their hopes for the future on the new US administration.

0:00 What foreign policy changes will Donald Trump implement? 
3:09 Ian Bremmer, Political Analyst and President of Eurasia Group

items.title: What capabilities do Russia and Ukraine have to attack deep into each other's territory? | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<html><head></head><body>Russia has once again launched dozens of long-range ballistic and cruise missiles, targeting Ukraine's national energy grid. Authorities said two critical infrastructure facilities were struck in the western region of Lviv, close to the Polish border. Emergency power cuts have been imposed in several regions. Kyiv also came under attack, with hundreds of residents taking shelter in underground metro stations across the capital. The attacks came just one day after Ukraine launched its largest-ever missile strikes on Russian infrastructure. An oil refinery, a fuel depot and an ammunition factory were among the targets hit, some of them around 1,000 kilometers inside Russian territory.  

For more news go to: http://www.dw.com/en/

items.title: Germany plans new 'takedown' legislation for unidentified drones as sightings increase | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<html><head></head><body>The German cabinet has approved plans to authorize the military to shoot down any drones deemed to be suspicious. This follows numerous drone sightings over critical military and industrial sites. Investigators believe they could be collecting information for possible acts of sabotage or terrorism - and Russia is the prime suspect.

items.title: AfD's Beatrix von Storch on Germany's energy infrastructure, economy, foreign policy and democracy
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<html><head></head><body>Germany's far-right AfD party wants no enlargement of the EU, least of all the accession of Ukraine, says Beatrix von Storch, a leading figure of the party. She also called for a halt to German weapon deliveries to Kyiv and an end to the fighting in Ukraine. Beatrix von Storch is a member of the German Bundestag and deputy leader of the AfD's parliamentary group there. DW's Matthew Moore talked to her at the Alternative for Germany's party conference in Riesa.

items.title: Why a ceasefire agreement between Israel and Hamas might be closer than ever before | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<html><head></head><body>Mediators in Qatar say Israel and Hamas are closing in on a ceasefire deal in Gaza. Another 17 people died overnight in Israeli air strikes, according to medics in Gaza.

items.title: LIVE: Marco Rubio testifies at Senate confirmation hearing for US Secretary of State | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<html><head></head><body>The US Senate Foreign Relations Committee holds a hearing to consider the nomination of Marc Rubio to become Secretary of State in the Trump administration. US President-elect Donald Trump's nominee for secretary of state Rubio is one of a trio of China hawks whom Donald Trump has tapped for senior roles, including Mike Waltz, who will be appointed Trump's national security adviser, and Elise Stefanik, nominated for ambassador to the United Nations. In 2018, Rubio co-sponsored the Reciprocal Access to Tibet Act, which seeks to deny entry into the United States to any Chinese officials who prohibit US citizens from entering Tibet, a territory claimed by China. He has also introduced legislation sanctioning China for its repression of the minority Uyghur population and the crackdown on protests in Hong Kong. The Chinese government responded by blacklisting Rubio.

#MarcoRubio #ConfirmationHearing #RubioHearing
For more news go to: http://www.dw.com/en/

items.title: Impeached South Korean president Yoon arrested after week-long standoff | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<html><head></head><body>Suspended South Korean president Yoon Suk Yeol has been detained for briefly imposing martial law in December. The former prosecutor could face life in jail or even the death penalty if found guilty. The Asian country's anti-corruption agency confirmed Yoon had been detained hours after hundreds of investigators and police officers arrived at his presidential compound to apprehend him. South Korea entered its worst political crisis in decades after Yoon sent soldiers to storm the parliament following his declaration of martial law.

For more news go to: http://www.dw.com/en/

items.title: How will history judge Joe Biden? | DW News Desk
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<html><head></head><body>As Joe Biden gets ready to hand over power to Donald Trump we look at the legacy Biden leaves behind. The 46th president of the United States entered the White House in the midst of Covid-19. Now, four years on, it’s time to reflect on the successes and failures of the Biden administration. From his pandemic response, to healthcare reforms, his handling of the economy and his migration policy. And overseas, the withdrawal from Afghanistan, his stance on China, and his support for Ukraine and Israel. What stands out for you when you think of the Joe Biden presidency?

Join us for a LIVE discussion on Wednesday at 08:00 (US - EDT) | 14:00 GMT | 15:00 (Europe/Central Africa Time) | 18:00 (Pakistan) | 18:30 (India) | 21:00 (Philippines)

items.title: Fact check: Is Trump’s cabinet on track to becoming a misinformation machine? | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<html><head></head><body>Donald Trump's political rise to a second term in the White House was accompanied by claims that he spread misinformation on issues like immigration or public health.

Key figures in his team are accused of pushing false narratives. And analysts fear this could erode trust in political institutions. 

DW Fact Check dives deep into these claims and their potential impact. 

DW contacted the Trump administration for comment but received no answer until publication date.

CHAPTERS:

00:00 Introduction: Elon Musk shares a fake story
00:59 A misinformation machine?
01:51 Disinformation and Trump’s health secretary
03:03 Exaggerated number of unaccompanied migrants
03:43 Political implications: Diminishing trust?
05:43 A troubling trend?

For more news go to: http://www.dw.com/en/

items.title: Cautious optimism for a ceasefire as the death toll continues to mount in Gaza | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<html><head></head><body>The terms of a deal between Israel and Hamas for a ceasefire in Gaza are close to agreement – that's according to US President Joe Biden.  

Negotiators are due to meet again later today in Doha to finalize a plan that would end the war as well as lead to the release of the hostages held in Gaza. Ceasefire talks have been taking place on and off for over a year and hopes for an agreement have been raised – and dashed – before. But now there is cautious optimism a breakthrough will happen this time.

A ceasefire agreement has eluded negotiators for months and DW's Benjamin Alvarez Gruber says there is skepticism in Washington that it will be achieved this time.

00:00 Death toll in Gaza mounts
02:40 DW's Benjamin Alvarez-Gruber

For more news go to: http://www.dw.com/en/

items.title: LA wildfires update: What to expect in the next 48 hours | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<html><head></head><body>Emergency crews battling to bring the Los Angeles wildfires under control are preparing for more dangerous weather. The National Weather Service has issued a rare warning of a "particularly dangerous situation" starting Monday night and continuing into Tuesday. 
The high winds that stoked the flames are forecast to return to Southern California. The fire chief in Los Angeles says the threat remains 'very high'. Several major fires continue to burn throughout the city and are only partly contained. At least 24 people have been confirmed dead.

items.title: Biden delivers final speech on US foreign policy | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<html><head></head><body>US president Joe Biden says a ceasefire in Gaza is 'on the brink' and that his administration was working to finalize a deal. 
He made the comments in his final foreign policy address at the State Department in Washington. Biden listed what he considered to be his achievements on the world stage including standing up to China and rallying 50 nations to stand with Ukraine in its war against Russia. On Gaza the President said the deal has the contours of his proposal he delivered last year.

items.title: German-Iranian activist released from Iran's notorious Evin prison | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<html><head></head><body>Nahid Taghavi, a German-Iranian activist, has been released from Iranian custody after more than four years. 70-year-old Taghavi was arrested during a trip to Tehran in 2020. She was sentenced to ten years in jail for allegedly spreading propaganda against the state and for involvement in an illegal group. She claims to have been tortured and held in solitary confinement for months.

items.title: Mozambique swears in new parliament despite protests and the opposition's ceremony boycott | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<html><head></head><body>There have been sporadic protests in Mozambique's capital Maputo as the new parliament was sworn into office on Monday. Mozambique has experienced deadly unrest since last October's disputed election. The security forces have been accused of using excessive force against demonstrators, who say the vote was rigged. President-elect Daniel Chapo has called for calm ahead of his inauguration on Wednesday, and the opposition has called for a strike in protest.  Chapo's Frelimo party has been in power for fifty years. 

DW's Dianne Hawker reports on protests in Maputo.

items.title: How much would it cost to fix Germany's infrastructure? | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<html><head></head><body>Germany's trains are late, bridges are broken and the military crucially underfunded. With elections coming up in six weeks, how much would any new government have to spend to fix these issues?  
In their published election manifestos, all parties suggest tax cuts that would shrink government income overall. While CDU/CSU, FDP and AfD only propose tax cuts, the SPD and Greens propose small tax increases on wealth and inheritance. The parties are split on the constitutionally enshrined debt-brake. CDU/CSU, FDP and AfD want to adhere to the rule, while SPD and Greens have suggested changing the rule and debt-financing infrastructure spending. 

#dwnewsshorts #debtbrake
#germanyinfrastructure

items.title: Do Kurds on Syrian border have reason to fear Turkish annexation? | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<html><head></head><body>It's been just over a month since Bashar al-Assad was toppled by Islamist-led rebel forces in Syria. Among the many groups hoping for greater representation in the new Syria is the country's Kurdish minority. During Syria's civil war, Syrian Democratic Forces - SDF - rebels fought to create a semi-autonomous area for the Kurdish population. 
About 10 percent of Syrians are ethnic Kurds. They're settled mostly in the north, along the border with Turkey, which has its own sizeable Kurdish minority.  The Syrian border city of Kobane is home to some 50,000 people, and local Kurds are increasingly fearful that Turkey could try to seize their city.

00:00 Kurds in Kobane fear a Turkish invasion
03:54 DW speaks with Hurcan Asli Aksoy, Head of the Centre for Applied Turkey Studies at the German Institute for International and Security Affairs

For more news go to: http://www.dw.com/en/

items.title: What were Joe Biden's foreign policy successes and failures? | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<html><head></head><body>US President Joe Biden is preparing to give a final foreign policy address in a few hours. He's expected to speak about the importance of alliances - and how they make the US stronger. And he'll tell Americans he left the country in better shape than when he took over from Donald Trump. It comes a week before he faces the task of handing the presidency back to him.

items.title: Kumbh Mela: The world's largest religious gathering | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<html><head></head><body>It's being billed as the biggest gathering of humanity in history. More than 400 million Hindu pilgrims are expected to take part in India's Maha Kumbh Mela religious festival. 
The celebration is held every 12 years in the city of Prayagraj in Uttar Pradesh state, where the Ganges and Yamuna rivers meet. Hindus believe that ritual bathing helps them gain salvation. DW's Adil Bhat followed one family's journey to the six-week festival.

For more news go to: http://www.dw.com/en/

items.title: Is South Korea's stalemate distracting it from bigger threats? | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<html><head></head><body>South Korea's suspended president Yoon Suk-Yeol is facing new hearings to decide on his future. Yoon has been barricaded in his hillside home, with crowds of supporters and his presidential guard blocking attempts to arrest him over his failed declaration of martial law. As the standoff drags on, there are concerns that wider problems are being neglected.
